exam.title: Information Security Awareness Training
exam.description:
This training assesses your understanding of everyday information security practices that keep our company, customers, and colleagues safe. It is designed for everyone — whether you work in engineering, operations, sales, HR, finance, or any other role.

You will encounter realistic scenarios covering passwords, phishing, social engineering, physical security, data handling, and incident response. Each question includes a short reading on the topic before you answer, so even if you are new to a concept you can learn it here and then apply it.

Pass mark: 70%. You are encouraged to retake the training periodically.
exam.instructions:
## Before you begin

- Read the **help text** shown before each question carefully. It contains the training content for that topic.
- Choose the **single best** answer unless the question states otherwise.
- You may take this exam at your own pace within the allotted time.
- If you are unsure, eliminate clearly wrong answers first.

## What to do if something goes wrong

- If you suspect a real security incident while taking this exam (e.g. you receive a suspicious email, you lose a device), **stop and contact the InfoSec team immediately** — do not wait until you finish.

questions.question: You receive an email from "IT Support" saying your mailbox is full and you will lose access in one hour unless you click a link and re-enter your password. The sender address is it-support@company-helpdesk.co (note the .co domain). What is the safest action?
questions.option_1: Click the link and re-enter the password so I don't lose email access.
questions.explanation_1: Clicking unverified links and entering credentials is exactly how phishing works.
questions.option_2: Forward the email to a colleague to ask what they think.
questions.explanation_2: Forwarding spreads the risk and may cause others to click. Report it instead.
questions.option_3: Report the email to the InfoSec / IT team using the 'Report Phishing' button (or forward to the reporting address), then delete it.
questions.explanation_3: Reporting helps the team block the campaign for everyone.
questions.option_4: Reply to the sender asking them to confirm if it is genuine.
questions.explanation_4: Attackers will happily reply 'yes, it's genuine'. Verify out-of-band, not by replying.
questions.help_text:
Spotting phishing emails

Phishing is the most common way attackers get into companies. The classic signals to watch for are:

  • Urgency or fear — 'you will lose access in 1 hour', 'your account will be closed'.
  • Lookalike sender domain — company-helpdesk.co instead of your real company.com.
  • A login link that asks for your password, especially when you didn't initiate the action.
  • Unexpected attachments, particularly ZIP, ISO, HTML, or macro-enabled Office files.

What to do: use the Report Phishing button in your mail client (or forward to security@). Do not click, do not reply. If the message claims to be from a real team, verify on a known channel (Slack DM, phone) — never by replying to the email.

questions.question: Which of these is the strongest approach to passwords for work accounts?
questions.option_1: A long unique passphrase per account, stored in an approved password manager, with multi-factor authentication (MFA) enabled.
questions.explanation_1: Length + uniqueness + MFA defeats credential stuffing and most attacks.
questions.option_2: One very complex password reused across all systems so it's easy to remember.
questions.explanation_2: Reuse means one breach compromises every account.
questions.option_3: Short passwords (8 characters) changed every 30 days.
questions.explanation_3: Modern guidance prefers length over forced rotation. Short passwords are weak regardless of how often they change.
questions.option_4: Writing passwords on a sticky note on the monitor for quick access.
questions.explanation_4: Physical exposure to anyone walking by; also visible on video calls.
questions.help_text:
Password hygiene

A strong password practice has three pillars:

  1. Length over complexity — a passphrase like correct-horse-battery-staple is stronger and easier to remember than P@ss1!.
  2. Unique per service — so a breach in one place can't be replayed elsewhere (credential stuffing).
  3. Use a password manager — the company-approved one. It generates and remembers passwords for you.

Always combine the above with multi-factor authentication (MFA) where available. MFA stops the vast majority of account-takeover attacks even when the password is leaked.

questions.question: Your phone is the second factor (MFA) for your work account. You suddenly start getting MFA push prompts you didn't trigger, one after another. What should you do?
questions.option_1: Approve one so the prompts stop — it must be a glitch.
questions.explanation_1: This is exactly the 'MFA fatigue' attack. Approving lets the attacker in.
questions.option_2: Deny all prompts and immediately report it to InfoSec; change your password.
questions.explanation_2: Repeated prompts mean someone already has your password and is trying to push you into approving.
questions.option_3: Turn off MFA so the prompts stop.
questions.explanation_3: Disabling MFA removes your last line of defence.
questions.option_4: Ignore it; the prompts will stop eventually.
questions.explanation_4: Even if you don't approve, the attacker now knows your password works.
questions.help_text:
MFA fatigue attacks

If an attacker has your password but is blocked by MFA, a common trick is to spam you with push notifications hoping you'll tap Approve just to make them stop — or in the middle of the night when you're half-asleep.

Rule: never approve a prompt you didn't trigger. A storm of prompts is itself a warning sign that your password is compromised. Deny, report, and rotate the password immediately.

questions.question: A caller says they are from the IT helpdesk, knows your name and team, and asks you to read out the 6-digit code that just popped up on your phone so they can 'verify your account'. What do you do?
questions.option_1: Read the code — they already know my name so they must be legitimate.
questions.explanation_1: Knowing your name proves nothing; it can be found on LinkedIn or the company directory.
questions.option_2: Refuse to share the code, end the call, and report the incident to InfoSec.
questions.explanation_2: Legitimate IT will never ask you to read out an MFA code. This is vishing.
questions.option_3: Read the code but tell them you find this suspicious.
questions.explanation_3: Once read, the attacker is in. Suspicion alone doesn't undo the damage.
questions.option_4: Ask them to call back from a different number to prove themselves.
questions.explanation_4: Caller IDs can be spoofed. A callback to an attacker's number proves nothing.
questions.help_text:
Vishing (voice phishing) and social engineering

Attackers can be friendly, professional, and well-informed. They may already know your name, manager, office location, or the project you're working on — gathered from LinkedIn, GitHub, or leaked data.

Golden rules:

  • No legitimate IT, bank, or vendor will ever ask you to read out an MFA code, OTP, or password.
  • If the request is urgent and unexpected, that is itself a signal to slow down.
  • Hang up and call back on a verified internal number (from the intranet, not from the caller).

questions.question: You're working from a coffee shop and need to access internal company systems. What is the right approach?
questions.option_1: Connect to the coffee shop Wi-Fi and log into systems directly — the websites are HTTPS so I'm safe.
questions.explanation_1: HTTPS protects content in transit but the network is still untrusted; also follow company policy.
questions.option_2: Use your phone's personal hotspot or the company VPN over the coffee shop Wi-Fi, per company policy.
questions.explanation_2: A hotspot or VPN gives you a trusted tunnel; you also follow the access policy.
questions.option_3: Look for an open Wi-Fi network called 'Free_Airport_Wifi' to save mobile data.
questions.explanation_3: Rogue hotspots with attractive names are a classic attacker trick.
questions.option_4: Disable your firewall to make the connection faster.
questions.explanation_4: Disabling security controls on an untrusted network is the opposite of what you should do.
questions.help_text:
Working on untrusted networks

Public Wi-Fi (cafes, airports, hotels) is shared with strangers. Even when sites use HTTPS, attackers on the same network can run rogue access points, intercept DNS, or push fake captive portals.

Safer choices, in order:

  1. Your phone's personal hotspot (most controlled).
  2. Public Wi-Fi with the company VPN on (creates an encrypted tunnel).

Never disable your laptop firewall or antivirus to 'make things work'. If something is blocked, raise a ticket.

questions.question: You leave your laptop unattended at your desk for a quick coffee break. What is the right thing to do?
questions.option_1: Lock the screen (Win+L on Windows, Ctrl+Cmd+Q on Mac) every time you step away, even for a minute.
questions.explanation_1: Locking takes one second and prevents tailgating attacks, pranks, and shoulder surfing.
questions.option_2: Leave it open — the office is trusted, my colleagues won't touch it.
questions.explanation_2: Visitors, contractors, cleaners, and tailgaters do enter offices. Insider mistakes also happen.
questions.option_3: Turn off the monitor only; that's enough.
questions.explanation_3: The session is still active — anyone can turn the monitor back on.
questions.option_4: Ask a nearby colleague to 'watch' it.
questions.explanation_4: Watching is not access control; they can't stop someone tapping a key.
questions.help_text:
Clean desk & lock screen

An unlocked workstation is a fully authenticated session for anyone who reaches the keyboard — including visitors, contractors, and tailgaters who slipped in behind someone.

Make screen-locking a reflex:

  • Windows: Win + L
  • Mac: Ctrl + Cmd + Q
  • Linux: Super + L (varies by distro)

Also clear sensitive papers off your desk at the end of the day — this is called a 'clean desk' policy.

questions.question: You find an unlabelled USB stick in the office parking lot. What should you do?
questions.option_1: Plug it into your work laptop to find out who it belongs to.
questions.explanation_1: This is a textbook 'drop attack'. The USB may run malware or act as a keyboard.
questions.option_2: Hand it to the InfoSec / IT team without plugging it in anywhere.
questions.explanation_2: InfoSec can examine it safely in a controlled environment.
questions.option_3: Plug it into your personal laptop, which is less important.
questions.explanation_3: Personal devices often connect back to work accounts. Same risk.
questions.option_4: Throw it in the bin to be safe.
questions.explanation_4: It might be a colleague's legitimate device, and InfoSec wants to know about drops.
questions.help_text:
USB drop attacks

Attackers leave USB sticks in parking lots, lobbies, and bathrooms hoping a curious employee will plug one in. Some are mass storage with malware; others are 'BadUSB' devices that pretend to be a keyboard and type commands the moment they're inserted.

Studies have shown ~45% of dropped USBs get plugged in. Don't be that 45%. Hand it to InfoSec.

questions.question: You urgently need to share a 200 MB file containing customer data with an external auditor. What's the right channel?
questions.option_1: Email it as an attachment to their personal Gmail address.
questions.explanation_1: Personal email is outside company control and often blocked by attachment-size limits anyway.
questions.option_2: Upload to the company-approved sharing platform with access restricted to the auditor's verified email, expiry date, and (where supported) password protection.
questions.explanation_2: Approved tools give you access control, auditability, and expiry.
questions.option_3: Put it on a USB stick and courier it.
questions.explanation_3: Physical media is easy to lose and harder to revoke. Use sanctioned tooling instead.
questions.option_4: Upload to a free public file-sharing site for convenience.
questions.explanation_4: Public services have no access controls and may retain data indefinitely.
questions.help_text:
Sharing sensitive data externally

When data leaves the company, you should be able to answer three questions:

  1. Who exactly can see it? (named, authenticated users)
  2. For how long? (expiring links)
  3. Can I revoke access if needed?

Approved tools (the corporate file-share, signed SFTP, etc.) answer all three. Personal email, consumer cloud, and random pastebins do not. When in doubt, ask InfoSec for the approved channel.

questions.question: Which of these counts as sensitive data that must not be pasted into a public chatbot or third-party AI service?
questions.option_1: Customer names and account numbers from a support ticket.
questions.explanation_1: Sensitive PII — but all three categories listed are sensitive, so the best single answer is 'all of the above'.
questions.option_2: Internal source code containing API keys.
questions.explanation_2: Source code and secrets are sensitive — but all three options listed qualify, so 'all of the above' is the best answer.
questions.option_3: Unpublished financial figures or strategic plans.
questions.explanation_3: Material non-public information is sensitive — but all three options qualify, so 'all of the above' is the best answer.
questions.option_4: All of the above.
questions.explanation_4: All three categories are sensitive and must not be sent to unapproved external services.
questions.help_text:
What counts as sensitive data

A useful mental model is to classify data into four tiers (the names may differ in your company):

  • Public — marketing pages, published policies.
  • Internal — org charts, internal wiki.
  • Confidential — customer PII, contracts, source code, financials before earnings.
  • Restricted / Regulated — payment data, government IDs, health data, credentials.

Confidential and Restricted data must never be pasted into public AI tools, free file-sharers, or personal accounts. If you're not sure of the classification, treat it as Confidential and ask.

questions.question: You suspect your work laptop has been compromised — you saw an unfamiliar terminal window flash open and now the machine feels slow. What is the first action?
questions.option_1: Run a virus scan and continue working while it scans.
questions.explanation_1: Continuing to work risks more damage and data exfiltration.
questions.option_2: Disconnect from the network (unplug Ethernet, turn off Wi-Fi) but leave the machine powered on, then contact InfoSec immediately.
questions.explanation_2: Disconnecting stops further data exfiltration; leaving it on preserves memory-resident evidence for the responders.
questions.option_3: Power off the machine immediately and reformat the drive.
questions.explanation_3: Reformatting destroys forensic evidence that responders need. Powering off wipes memory.
questions.option_4: Wait a few hours to see if the problem goes away.
questions.explanation_4: Every minute of delay can mean more data lost or more systems infected.
questions.help_text:
Responding to a suspected compromise

If you think a device has been hacked, the goals are: contain, preserve, report.

  1. Contain: disconnect from the network (unplug Ethernet, turn off Wi-Fi). This stops the attacker reaching out.
  2. Preserve: leave the device powered on. Volatile memory (RAM) contains evidence that helps responders. Do not reboot, reformat, or delete files.
  3. Report: contact the InfoSec team immediately via the established channel.

For active security incidents, contact cybersec@zerodha.com before taking destructive steps.

questions.question: You receive a Slack DM from someone whose profile photo looks like your CEO. The message says: 'I'm in a meeting and need you to urgently buy $500 of gift cards for a client gift — will reimburse. Please don't tell anyone yet.' What is the right response?
questions.option_1: Buy the gift cards quickly — the CEO doesn't ask twice.
questions.explanation_1: This is a classic 'CEO gift card scam'. Never act on financial urgency without verification.
questions.option_2: Reply asking for more details so you can be sure.
questions.explanation_2: The impersonator will happily reply. Verify out-of-band, not on the same channel.
questions.option_3: Verify the request out-of-band (e.g. video call, in person, or a phone number you already have for the CEO) and report the message to InfoSec.
questions.explanation_3: Out-of-band verification defeats impersonation. Reporting helps protect colleagues too.
questions.option_4: Forward the request to a colleague to handle on your behalf.
questions.explanation_4: You've now spread the scam to someone who may act on it.
questions.help_text:
Executive impersonation & business email compromise (BEC)

Attackers create fake profiles or compromise real accounts to impersonate executives. The pattern is consistent:

  • Authority (CEO, CFO).
  • Urgency ('right now', 'I'm in a meeting').
  • Secrecy ('don't tell anyone yet').
  • An irreversible payment (gift cards, crypto, wire transfer).

Any one of those is a warning. All four together is a confirmed scam. Verify on a separate, trusted channel every time, even if it feels awkward. Real executives expect — and prefer — this caution.

questions.question: Which of the following is a sign that a website's login page might be malicious?
questions.option_1: The URL is a near-miss of the real one, e.g. https://accounts.goog1e.com (with a digit 1 in 'google').
questions.explanation_1: Typosquatting / homoglyph domains are a hallmark of phishing.
questions.option_2: The page uses HTTPS and shows a padlock icon.
questions.explanation_2: HTTPS only proves the connection is encrypted; attackers obtain certificates routinely.
questions.option_3: The page asks for your username and password.
questions.explanation_3: Legitimate login pages do this too. By itself it's not suspicious.
questions.option_4: The page is loading slowly.
questions.explanation_4: Slow loading is annoying but not a security signal.
questions.help_text:
Checking URLs before you log in

Before entering credentials, look at the address bar — specifically the domain, the part immediately before the first single /.

Common tricks:

  • Typosquatting: goog1e.com, micros0ft.com.
  • Subdomain confusion: company.com.attacker.net — the real domain is attacker.net.
  • Homoglyphs: Cyrillic letters that look identical to Latin ones.

A padlock icon means encrypted, not trustworthy. When in doubt, navigate to the site by typing the known URL yourself or via a saved bookmark — don't click the email link.

questions.question: You're using your personal phone for work email and Slack (BYOD). The phone is lost or stolen. What should you do?
questions.option_1: Wait 24 hours to see if it turns up before doing anything.
questions.explanation_1: Delay gives attackers time to access work data and messages.
questions.option_2: Report it immediately to InfoSec/IT and ask for the work profile to be remote-wiped; change passwords for work accounts.
questions.explanation_2: Fast reporting limits damage; remote wipe protects work data even if the rest of the phone is lost.
questions.option_3: Only change the lock screen PIN from another device.
questions.explanation_3: If the phone is already unlocked or sessions are active, a PIN change doesn't help.
questions.option_4: Post about it on social media so the finder can return it.
questions.explanation_4: Sharing details publicly can attract attackers and doesn't reduce risk.
questions.help_text:
Lost or stolen devices

For any device with work access — laptop, phone, tablet — the moment you realise it's missing is the moment to act:

  1. Report to InfoSec/IT immediately. Most companies can remote-wipe the work profile without touching personal photos.
  2. Rotate passwords on accounts the device was logged into.
  3. Sign out the device from those accounts from another machine.

Speed matters: most attackers exploit a stolen device within hours, not days.

questions.question: You see a 'critical security update' popup on your work laptop. What is the safest behaviour?
questions.option_1: Click 'Install now' on any popup that mentions security.
questions.explanation_1: Fake update popups are a common malware delivery channel, especially in the browser.
questions.option_2: Only install updates through the operating system's built-in updater or the company's device-management agent; ignore popups that appear in a browser tab.
questions.explanation_2: Legitimate OS updates do not arrive via random browser popups.
questions.option_3: Postpone all updates indefinitely — updates break things.
questions.explanation_3: Unpatched systems are the most common way attackers get in.
questions.option_4: Download an updater from a Google search result.
questions.explanation_4: Search results frequently include malicious ads impersonating real software.
questions.help_text:
Software updates

Keeping software patched is one of the single most effective defences against attacks. But how you update matters:

  • OS updates: Windows Update, macOS Software Update, or your company's MDM agent.
  • App updates: use the app's own built-in updater or the official app store.
  • Never: install software from random popups or sponsored search results.

Fake 'your browser is out of date' popups are one of the most common malware vectors. The real update never arrives as a popup inside a web page.

questions.question: A new colleague holds the secure office door open behind you so a person you don't recognise can follow you in — the person says 'thanks, forgot my badge'. What should you do?
questions.option_1: Politely ask them to badge in themselves or to check in at reception; if they can't, alert security/reception.
questions.explanation_1: This prevents tailgating without being confrontational; reception can validate them.
questions.option_2: Say nothing — it's not your responsibility, they're already in.
questions.explanation_2: Physical security is everyone's responsibility. Tailgating is a leading cause of unauthorised access.
questions.option_3: Lecture your colleague in front of the stranger.
questions.explanation_3: Embarrassing colleagues isn't necessary; act calmly toward the unverified person.
questions.option_4: Assume they're a contractor and let them through.
questions.explanation_4: Assumption is the gap attackers rely on. Verification through reception is the only safe path.
questions.help_text:
Tailgating & physical access

Tailgating is when an unauthorised person follows an authorised one through a secure door. Most office breaches in the physical world start this way, not by picking locks.

The polite-but-firm script: 'Hey, please badge in yourself — or check in at reception, they'll sort you out.'

You are not being rude; you are doing the job the badge system exists for. Real visitors expect this.

questions.question: You're disposing of an old work laptop. What should you do?
questions.option_1: Drop it in the recycling bin so it gets processed.
questions.explanation_1: Recyclers won't sanitise data; the drive remains readable.
questions.option_2: Just delete personal files and leave the OS installed.
questions.explanation_2: Deleted files are recoverable. Full disk wipe or destruction is required.
questions.option_3: Return it to the IT asset team for secure wipe or physical destruction following company policy.
questions.explanation_3: IT follows a documented sanitisation procedure (cryptographic erase, certified wipe, or shredding).
questions.option_4: Sell it on a second-hand marketplace after a normal format.
questions.explanation_4: A normal format does not erase data; corporate assets must follow disposal policy.
questions.help_text:
Secure disposal of devices & media

Company-owned devices and media (laptops, phones, USBs, paper) often retain data even after a normal delete or format. They must be returned to IT/InfoSec for sanitisation following policy — typically a certified disk wipe, cryptographic erase, or physical destruction (shredding for paper and disks).

This includes external drives, backup media, and printed sensitive documents.

questions.question: You're on a train and need to review a confidential customer document on your laptop. The person next to you keeps glancing at your screen. What is the best mitigation?
questions.option_1: Use a physical privacy filter screen and/or angle the laptop away; defer reviewing the most sensitive parts until you're in a private space.
questions.explanation_1: Privacy filters narrow viewing angles; combined with situational awareness this is the practical answer.
questions.option_2: Make the font tiny so the seatmate can't read it.
questions.explanation_2: Small font is still readable to a determined shoulder-surfer; you can't measure that risk.
questions.option_3: Ask the seatmate to look away.
questions.explanation_3: There's no obligation for them to comply, and you've drawn attention to the document.
questions.option_4: Carry on as normal; trains are public spaces and that's just how it is.
questions.explanation_4: Confidentiality obligations don't pause in public.
questions.help_text:
Shoulder surfing

Working in public — trains, flights, cafes, co-working spaces — means your screen and conversations are visible to strangers.

Mitigations:

  • Privacy filter: a thin physical screen overlay that limits viewing angles. Cheap and effective.
  • Positioning: sit with your back to a wall.
  • Defer the sensitive parts: not every task needs to happen on the train.
  • Conversations: assume nearby strangers can hear you on calls.

questions.question: What is ransomware and what is the most reliable defence against it for an individual employee?
questions.option_1: Malware that encrypts files and demands payment; the best individual defence is to not open suspicious attachments/links and to keep systems updated.
questions.explanation_1: Most ransomware enters via phishing attachments or unpatched software. Personal vigilance plus patching covers the majority of risk.
questions.option_2: A type of antivirus software that protects against demands.
questions.explanation_2: The name describes the attack, not a defence.
questions.option_3: Hardware that ransoms physical access to the office.
questions.explanation_3: Ransomware is software-based, not hardware-based.
questions.option_4: Paying the ransom is always the safest option.
questions.explanation_4: Paying funds attackers, doesn't guarantee data recovery, and may have legal/sanctions implications.
questions.help_text:
Ransomware

Ransomware is malware that encrypts your files (and often, exfiltrates copies first) and demands payment for the decryption key. Most ransomware enters companies through:

  • Phishing attachments (Office macros, ZIPs, HTML smugglers).
  • Unpatched internet-facing software.
  • Stolen credentials reused across services.

As an individual, your most effective defences are:

  1. Don't open suspicious links/attachments — report them.
  2. Keep your OS and apps updated via approved channels.
  3. Use MFA so leaked passwords are not enough.
  4. Store work files on the approved corporate location so they are backed up — if your local machine is ransomed, IT can recover.

questions.question: You discover that a colleague is sharing one shared login (same username and password) for a critical system among five people, 'so we don't have to wait for IT to provision each of us'. What is the right action?
questions.option_1: Join in — if everyone is doing it, it must be fine.
questions.explanation_1: Shared accounts break accountability and auditability and likely violate policy.
questions.option_2: Privately raise it with your manager or InfoSec so each person gets their own account and the shared password is rotated.
questions.explanation_2: This restores individual accountability without blaming colleagues publicly.
questions.option_3: Ignore it — it's not your team.
questions.explanation_3: Security is a shared responsibility; consequences of a breach affect everyone.
questions.option_4: Post the password on a team wiki so it's easier to find.
questions.explanation_4: That increases exposure and creates an audit trail of the violation.
questions.help_text:
Why shared accounts are dangerous

Shared credentials feel convenient but create three problems:

  1. No accountability: if something goes wrong, no one knows who did what.
  2. No least-privilege: everyone has every permission the shared account has.
  3. Stale access: when someone leaves the team, the password is rarely rotated — so they keep access.

Each person should have their own account with the minimum access they need. For service accounts (used by software, not humans), follow the company's secret-management process — never paste the password in a wiki or Slack channel.

questions.question: The InfoSec team sends an annual reminder asking everyone to report any suspicious email, even if they didn't click anything. Why does reporting matter?
questions.option_1: It blames the sender so they get into trouble.
questions.explanation_1: Reporting is about defence, not blame — the sender is usually a stranger or an impersonator.
questions.option_2: Each report helps InfoSec block the campaign for everyone else, identify trends, and warn colleagues before they fall for it.
questions.explanation_2: One report can prevent dozens of compromises across the company.
questions.option_3: Only IT staff should report; ordinary employees shouldn't bother.
questions.explanation_3: Most reports come from regular employees — you are the front line of detection.
questions.option_4: Reports are deleted unread, so it makes no difference.
questions.explanation_4: Reports feed real detection rules and threat intelligence.
questions.help_text:
Why reporting matters

Modern phishing campaigns send the same lure to many employees. The first person to report it lets InfoSec:

  • Block the sender domain at the email gateway.
  • Pull copies from other inboxes before colleagues open them.
  • Identify affected accounts if anyone did click, and lock them down.

You don't need to be certain — report when in doubt. False positives are welcome; missed reports are how breaches start. There is never a downside to reporting.
